metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Entitlements / CPU Access
Technical Action Required: Yes (entitlement check)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on a Tier2 GPU project requested CPU-only runs on icelake. The correct response is to first verify current CPU account entitlements (e.g. via `mybalance`) before advising which account to use.

ACTION
1) Verify user’s current account entitlements (`mybalance` or equivalent account view).
2) If an SL3 CPU account is present, instruct user to submit CPU jobs with that account.
3) If no CPU account is present, provide the correct request/provisioning path.
4) Clarify SL2 path for larger/longer CPU requirements where applicable.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

Please check your available project accounts with:
`mybalance`

If you see an SL3 CPU account there, you can use that for CPU-only runs on the relevant CPU partition.

If you need larger/longer CPU resources than SL3 allows (or no CPU account is listed), please request the appropriate SL2 CPU access via your group lead.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Confirm actual entitlement list before advising new provisioning.
metrics.user_input:
Dear HPC support,
I am a PhD student with Pietro Lio project cs181 which has access to the Ampere node partition.
I have a need for some CPU only runs on the icelake nodes and was wondering if there's anyway to get this?
Many thanks
James Rowbottom - jr908
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 09 December 2024 17:25
To: James Rowbottom <jr908@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: New Tier2 Account on CSD3 (jr908)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Before you login for the first time, please use your SSH
public key to login via SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
Once MFA is configured, please use your SSH public key to
login via SSH to
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The scratch storage has been implemented as part of the Cambridge
Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under ~/rds/ for your
project's scratch directory (rds-t2-cs181). Please note that
this is shared per project so each user will probably wish to create
their own subdirectory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjr908%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7Cb2a72e3346134cb8757b08dd187683f7%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638693619533696586%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=GUPkCeyukD4EZY8jc2oXOIpFpIpHahjHvOyx2dOSqx4%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The project listed by mybalance is the project you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A t2-cs181-XXX
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A t2-cs181-XXX ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for DiRAC jobs are currently:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Account Entitlements / CPU Access
Technical Action Required: Yes (entitlement check)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User on a Tier2 GPU project requested CPU-only runs on icelake. The correct response is to first verify current CPU account entitlements (e.g. via `mybalance`) before advising which account to use.

ACTION
1) Verify user’s current account entitlements (`mybalance` or equivalent account view).
2) If an SL3 CPU account is present, instruct user to submit CPU jobs with that account.
3) If no CPU account is present, provide the correct request/provisioning path.
4) Clarify SL2 path for larger/longer CPU requirements where applicable.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi James,

Please check your available project accounts with:
`mybalance`

If you see an SL3 CPU account there, you can use that for CPU-only runs on the relevant CPU partition.

If you need larger/longer CPU resources than SL3 allows (or no CPU account is listed), please request the appropriate SL2 CPU access via your group lead.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Confirm actual entitlement list before advising new provisioning.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Resilience / Checkpointing Policy
Technical Action Required: No (capability clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User asked about Slurm native `--checkpoint` functionality. Platform does not provide native Slurm checkpoint plugin support; users must implement checkpointing at application/workflow level. `dmtcp` module exists but compatibility with AlphaFold is user-dependent.

ACTION
1) Clarify no native Slurm checkpoint plugin on this cluster.
2) Recommend user-level checkpoint/restart implementation.
3) Mention `dmtcp` as optional tool to evaluate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Lisa,

Native Slurm checkpoint/restart is not enabled on this cluster, so `--checkpoint` options are not available here.

Checkpointing must be implemented at application/workflow level. We do provide a `dmtcp` module, though compatibility with AlphaFold would need to be tested in your workflow.

Kind regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply Slurm-native checkpoint flags are supported when plugin is absent.

Dear HPC Support team,
could I receive advice on using checkpoints when running a slurm script for AlphaFold 2.3.2?
I have found the following documentation useful:[https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html](https://cluster.hpcc.ucr.edu/~jhayes/slurm/16.05.4/checkpoint_blcr.html)
sbatch
Several options have been added to support checkpoint restart:
- --checkpoint: Specifies the interval between periodic checkpoint of a batch job. By default, the job will have no checkpoints created. Acceptable time formats include "minutes", "minutes:seconds", "hours:minutes:seconds", "days-hours", "days-hours:minutes" and "days-hours:minutes:seconds".
- --checkpoint-dir:Specify the directory when the checkpoint image files of a batch job will be stored. The default value is the current working directory. Checkpoint files will be of the form "<job_id>.ckpt" for jobs and "<job_id>.<step_id>.ckpt" for job steps.
Environment variables are available for all of these options:
SLURM_CHECKPOINT is equivalent to --checkpoint:
- SLURM_CHECKPOINT_DIR is equivalent to --checkpoint-dir
however, the --checkpoint command does not work from a script of from terminal. According to the slurm-rest-23.02 update log (which I believe is the current version of slurm on HPC):
"* The checkpoint plugin interface and all associated API calls have been removed."
Does this mean it is not possible to use checkpoints on HPC?
Thank you for your help,
Lisa

metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Singularity / AlphaFold3
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing a "FATAL: permission denied" error when trying to run the AlphaFold3 Singularity container [1]. This issue has been encountered by other users in the past, and the solution typically involves modifying the run script or adjusting the permissions [1][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, the user should try deleting the existing run_alphafold3 script and creating a new one with the following content:
```
#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/3

singularity exec --env \
  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \
  -B $DATA:/data \
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \
  --nv ${SIMAGE} \
  python run_alphafold.py \
  --db_dir=/data
  "$@" 
```
Then, make the script executable with `chmod +x ./run_alphafold3` [4].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the permission denied error, please try creating a new run_alphafold3 script with the provided content and make it executable. If you continue to experience issues, please provide more details about the error message and the steps you have taken so far.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Containers / AlphaFold / Singularity
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The AlphaFold3 container appears to have been created, but the user gets `FATAL: permission denied` when running `./run_alphafold3`. From the visible query alone, the safest interpretation is that the generated launcher script, its execute permissions, or the way it invokes the container needs checking. The next step is to inspect the wrapper script, confirm it is executable, and verify the image/data paths being used.

ACTION
1) Ask the user to show the generated launcher script and its permissions, e.g.:
   - `ls -l run_alphafold3`
   - `head -n 40 run_alphafold3`
2) Confirm the container image exists in the install directory and is readable.
3) If the launcher is not executable, instruct the user to run `chmod +x run_alphafold3`.
4) Review whether the launcher is invoking Singularity correctly for the local installation.
5) Confirm the required AlphaFold data/model weights are available and mapped as documented.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Daniel,

The `permission denied` error suggests the generated launcher script or the way it is being invoked needs checking.

Please send us:
- the output of `ls -l run_alphafold3`
- the first part of the script (`head -n 40 run_alphafold3`)
- confirmation that the AlphaFold image file exists in the install directory

If the launcher is not executable, please also try:
`chmod +x run_alphafold3`

Once we can see the wrapper contents and permissions, we can confirm the correct Singularity invocation.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume the container image is broken if the wrapper script was generated incorrectly.
- For AlphaFold3, confirm that model weights access has been approved separately.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Singularity / AlphaFold3
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing a "FATAL: permission denied" error when trying to run the AlphaFold3 Singularity container [1]. This issue has been encountered by other users in the past, and the solution typically involves modifying the run script or adjusting the permissions [1][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve the issue, the user should try deleting the existing run_alphafold3 script and creating a new one with the following content:
```
#!/bin/bash
SIMAGE=/alphafold3_3.0.1.sif
DATA=/datasets/public/AlphaFold/3

singularity exec --env \
  TF_FORCE_UNIFIED_MEMORY=1,XLA_PYTHON_CLIENT_MEM_FRACTION=4.0,OPENMM_CPU_THREADS=32 \
  -B $DATA:/data \
  -B .:/etc
  --pwd /app/alphafold \
  --nv ${SIMAGE} \
  python run_alphafold.py \
  --db_dir=/data
  "$@" 
```
Then, make the script executable with `chmod +x ./run_alphafold3` [4].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the permission denied error, please try creating a new run_alphafold3 script with the provided content and make it executable. If you continue to experience issues, please provide more details about the error message and the steps you have taken so far.
Best regards,
HPC Support

Additionally, users should be aware of the licensing terms and conditions for AlphaFold3 and ensure that they comply with the requirements [2][3].

Marti

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Installation / Central Module Provisioning
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants help running BerkeleyGW 4.0 on Ampere GPU nodes but does not have experience building it there. The best initial support path is to check whether a supported central build/module already exists and, if not, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team rather than relying on the user to complete a complex manual GPU build unaided.

ACTION
1) Confirm the exact version/variant required.
2) Check whether a supported central BerkeleyGW module already exists for Ampere.
3) If a supported module exists, send the user the relevant module-load instructions.
4) If no module exists, gather the exact build requirements and escalate to the software/RSE team.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Bingcan,

We will check whether there is already a supported BerkeleyGW build/module available for Ampere.

If there is, we will send you the exact load commands. If not, we will need the precise version and build requirements so that we can advise on the next step or escalate appropriately.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Compilation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeking assistance with compiling BerkeleyGW on CSD3, specifically for exciton calculations on NVIDIA nodes [1]. A similar issue was previously resolved by loading specific modules and using an attached arch.mk file [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To compile BerkeleyGW on CSD3, follow these steps:
1. Load the necessary modules: `module purge`, `module load rhel8/cclake/base`, `module load intel-oneapi-compilers/2023.2.4/gcc/4lbvg4hv`, `module load hdf5/1.14.3/intel/intel-oneapi-mpi/si2tlq4v`, and `module load intel-oneapi-mkl-cluster/2024.1.0/intel/r55ddo3z` [1].
2. Use the provided arch.mk file to compile BerkeleyGW [1].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Dear Bingcan,
To compile BerkeleyGW on CSD3 for exciton calculations, please load the necessary modules and use the attached arch.mk file as described in the previous resolution [1]. If you encounter any issues, please provide the error messages for further assistance.

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the necessary modules are loaded before compiling BerkeleyGW [1].
- Use the provided arch.mk file to avoid compatibility issues [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-90945

[SUMMARY] Compiling Quantum Espresso on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi there,  
I have been using Quantum Espresso (v6.4 or v6.3) on CSD3 for a while. For the electron-phonon calculations, I realized that a part of the code is not parallized and I modified one of the subroutines and parallelized it with MPI on another machine. The calculations that took 50 hours before are now taking around 4 hours when use 20 cores. (It's not perfect but it is much better)  
I haven't tested my implementation on other machines or with multiple nodes. However, I want to try to implement it on CSD3 since I have less than 3 weeks for my MPhil thesis submission and I need to run some heavy calculations.  
I will be glad if you can help me compile it on my home directory or as a module. The subroutine I am changing is at "/PHonon/PH/elphon.f90" script inside QE directories. I attached my modified script.  
Looking forward to hear back from you.  
Best regards, 
Kem 
  
--- 
Kemal Atalar

Cavendish Laboratory 
University of Cambridge 
Cambridge 3B3 0HE

<MESSAGE id=0004 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi Jeffrey,
I haven't try compiling it yet. I am trying it now. I used the following flags previously when compiling:
./configure --prefix=${HOME}/QE  MPIF90=mpifort \
F90=ifort F77=ifort BLAS_LIBS=-mkl=sequential LAPACK_LIBS=-mkl=sequential
Should I use the same ones on CSD3 or is there a more suitable configuration?
Best regards,
Kem
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0005 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
That should be a fine start. We prefer the ELPA libraries (with --with-elpa options to tell it where elpa is installed) for large-scale problems but it generally doesn't make a great deal of difference. 
Let me know if you run into errors and we will do our best to help. 
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0006 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
When I try this configuration, I get some warnings:
configure: WARNING: F90 value is set to be consistent with value of MPIF90
checking for mpifort... no
configure: WARNING: MPIF90 not found: using MPIF90 anyway
checking version of mpifort... unknown, assuming gfortran
checking for Fortran flag to compile .f90 files... unknown
configure: error: Fortran could not compile .f90 files
Should I change the MPIF90 option to something else?
Best,
Kem
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0007 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Ah yes, `mpifort` is not a wrapper provided by Intel MPI. Unfortunately there is no consensus on what fortran compilers should be called - fort, f90, fc are all used. In this case you can use mpif90 rather than mpifort.
Best wishes,
Jeffrey.
</MESSAGE>

No problem, good luck with your project!

Jeffrey.

benchmark_annotation.summary: Installing level-zero for multi-xpu training with Intel XPU

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN / Intel XPU / oneAPI Modules
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user assumed they needed `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` for DAWN XPU work. On DAWN, the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes, so the supported first step is to use the provided module stack rather than trying to install system packages with `sudo`.

ACTION
1) Explain that the node drivers should already be present.
2) Ask whether they are using a container or virtual environment.
3) Suggest loading the provided module:
   `module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`
4) If they still prefer a user-space install, confirm they can do that in their own environment rather than system-wide.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Jiangnan,

You should not need to use `sudo apt install level-zero-dev` on DAWN, because the Level Zero drivers are already present on the nodes.

Please first try:
`module load oneapi-level-zero/1.14/gcc/czctrcfw`

If you are building inside a container or a virtual environment, please let us know which approach you are using so we can advise more precisely.

Best regards,
HPC Support

Good morning,
I’ve managed to get a large proportion of our code codes ported to GPU using JAX, an appropriate framework for our needs, which shows excellent performance at large scale.
It would be fantastic if these calculations could be run on DAWN. However, as documented in this thread, I have not had any success in installing it.
I understand there is no reason why it should not work on Intel machines, see the stable diffusion example below which is run on a similar hardware configuration as DAWN.
[https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html)
It would be great if I could have some support in doing this, as I think our group and other potential JAX/DAWN users will benefit greatly from this.
Many thanks,
Lewis

The following simple python script should work:

```
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex
count = torch.xpu.device_count()
print(f"✅ Found {count} XPU device(s).")
for i in range(count):
print(f" - Device {i}: {torch.xpu.get_device_name(i)}")
model = torch.nn.Linear(10, 5)
model = model.to("xpu")
model.eval()

# Optimize with IPEX
model = ipex.optimize(model)

x = torch.randn(1, 10).to(“xpu”)
with torch.no_grad():
y = model(x)
print("Model output:", y)
```

Matt.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos

Portal Action Required: No [1]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with setting up and using Python environments on CSD3, including slow performance and freezing when importing packages like torch [1]. The user has tried using their own installation of micromamba, as well as the pre-installed miniconda3, but is still experiencing problems [1]. Similar issues have been reported in the past, with possible causes including filesystem problems and network I/O bottlenecks [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Investigate the user's environment setup and package installation process to identify potential causes of the issue [1].
2. Check the filesystem and network I/O performance to see if there are any ongoing issues that could be contributing to the problem [1][2].
3. Consider recommending alternative package managers or environment setup methods, such as using the module system or pre-installed packages [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about your environment setup and package installation process?
- Have you tried running your Python scripts on a different node or with a different environment setup?
- Are there any specific error messages or output that you are seeing when you encounter the issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with your Python environment setup. We are investigating the problem and will get back to you with further instructions or recommendations.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when recommending alternative package managers or environment setup methods, as they may have different compatibility or security implications [1][3].
- Ensure that any changes to the user's environment setup or package installation process are thoroughly tested and validated before implementation [1].

metrics.user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Slow Conda environment setup and activation on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
I am a PhD student in the Engineering Department, division F, have been experiencing some significant delays with conda environment setup and activation on CSD3, and am attaching details below which I hope can be helpful in diagnosing the issue.
1. Environment build failures
I had to make three attempts to build a conda environment with PyTorch 2.8.0 and pyannote.audio 4.0.3. The first two jobs timed out despite using very little CPU or memory. The bottleneck appears to be network I/O when downloading packages. Here are the details:
Job ID
State
Wall-clock
CPU Used
Memory Used
21592679
TIMEOUT (killed during "Verifying transaction")
02:00:11
00:01:22 (0.28%)
357 MB (2.18%)
21596158
TIMEOUT (killed during "Installing pip dependencies")
04:00:08
00:02:00 (0.21%)
508 MB (3.10%)
21629759
COMPLETED
00:07:36
00:01:17 (3.38%)
278 MB (1.70%)
The third job completed quickly because the packages were already cached from the previous two attempts.
2. Conda activation and script runtime speed
Since last Thursday, activating conda environment has been notably slow. I had to remove the conda initialisation lines from my ~/.bashrc file in order to access the login node. I also tried requesting an interactive session and a CPU cluster node, but it could take 10–20 minutes to activate a conda environment. Things have been better today (around 3–5 minutes), but I thought it was still worth reporting in case the issue recurs.
Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

[TICKET_SUMMARY] CSD3 Issues

[CONTEXT]
 I have been running some tests and, although I do see improvements, it is still too slow for my workflow.
e.g. running on gpu-q-3 in interactive mode (sintr -A MLMI-lv380-SL2-GPU -p ampere --gres=gpu:1 -N1 -n1 -t 1:00:0 --qos=INTR)
>conda activate base
>python
>import torch
Takes several minutes
Best regards,
Leonhard

[CONVERSATION]

On login-q-4 which takes about 3 minutes to come back to me.
I hope this issue can get resolved.

Best,
Leonhard

Hello Leonhard,

Elisabeth

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Cannot set-up conda environment on CSD3 HPC

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear CSD3 team,
I have encountered a hurdle when trying to set-up and run computations on your cluster for the first time.
I am unable to successfully set-up my Conda environment with the packages that I require to run my Python script. I have been working to resolve this issue for multiple days now but at no avail.
Initially, I started by trying to re-create the Conda environment that I currently work on within a different  compute cluster. When this did not work, I simply tried installing one package at a time, but would still encounter the same issue regardless of how simple I tried to make the process.
The error I keep encountering (many times, sometimes in slightly differing error messages) is that the environment is inconsistent and it fails to resolve. Here are the packages that I need to install to allow my code to run:
bctpy – Brain Connectivity Toolbox (Python version; installable via pip)
*
h5py
*
matplotlib
*
networkx
*
numpy
*
pandas
*
plotly
*
scikit-learn
*
scipy
*
seaborn
*
torch
*
umap-learn
I have tried different solutions including updating conda (was not able to do this, same issue) and installing mamba (was not able to do this, same issue). I am not very knowledgeable about these things - but I have been able to work with conda successfully on another compute cluster, no issue.
I would very much appreciate any help that you could give me. I am available at any time to provide any more information, or to work through this through videocall/screenshare if this is something that you do.
Thanks and best wishes,
El

Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment Performance / Shared Storage
Technical Action Required: No (performance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo

HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

I have recently been trying to connect to the CSD3 to start working in the cluster for the first time. I have Two-Factor Authentication on my account already enabled (please see image attached).
When I try to connect to the login node (login.hpc.cam.ac.uk) through ssh I am being requested to provide the TOTP Verification Code. Unfortunately, I did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Would it be possible to reset this multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code so I can scan it with Google Authenticator to set the login for my account?
Many thanks for your assistance on this matter.

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC Cluster Access / MFA Setup
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with setting up their TOTP MFA secret for the HPC cluster [1]. They have tried to connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk but are unable to generate the QR code [1]. The user has also mentioned that they are using Microsoft Authenticator and Google Authenticator, but neither can read the QR code [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, we need to verify the user's identity and reset their MFA secret [2]. We can do this by scheduling a video call with the user to verify their identity [2]. Once verified, we can reset their MFA secret and provide them with a new QR code to scan [2].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please confirm your CRSid and UIS password to verify your identity?
- Have you tried using a different authenticator app to scan the QR code?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your email. I have tried to connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk but am unable to generate the QR code. I am using Microsoft Authenticator and Google Authenticator, but neither can read the QR code. Please let me know the next steps to resolve this issue.
Best,
[User Name]

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- We must verify the user's identity before resetting their MFA secret [2].
- We should ensure that the user is using a compatible authenticator app [1].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-52352
[2] : HPCSSUP-60021

[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7,
OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Peta4 clusters. login-icelake will take you to a
different set of RHEL8, Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes
best suited for development work for Ice Lake and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
    MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
    SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
    MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
    SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on skylake* and cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk. 
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see 
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.)](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.))
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU 
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
 
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
 
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
 
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
 
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
 
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

benchmark_annotation.summary: Unable to log back into R Studio during interactive Session

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RStudio / Interactive Session
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with connecting to RStudio Server while running an interactive session on the HPC [1]. The error message "Temporary server error, please try again" is displayed when attempting to connect to the RStudio Server [4]. Similar issues have been reported in the past, and resolving the issue often involves trying a new session or checking the settings used to start the interactive session [1][3][4].

ACTION
Try requesting a new session to see if the issue persists [4]. If the problem continues, please share the settings used to start the interactive session, including the partition, number of hours, number of nodes, and any additional modules loaded [1][3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please share a screenshot of the error message you are seeing when attempting to connect to the RStudio Server?
- Have you tried using the default modules (R and rstudio-server) when starting the interactive session [3]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your assistance. I have tried requesting a new session, and the issue still persists. I am using the default partition "desktop" and the default modules (R and rstudio-server). I have attached a screenshot of the error message I am seeing.
Best regards,
[User]

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When experiencing issues with RStudio Server, try requesting a new session before seeking further assistance [4].
- Always use the default modules (R and rstudio-server) when starting an interactive session unless otherwise specified [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-54003
[3] : HPCSSUP-74123
[4] : HPCSSUP-88569
metrics.user_input: During my interactive session with RStudio, I noticed that if I am not active on the page while my model is running, I am logged out of the RStudio server, but the login I use for the HPC does not work to log back into the RStudio server. I have attempted to refresh the HPC main page, log out and back in, and then relaunch my RStudio session that is currently running. However, when I try to relaunch it, I am redirected to an RStudio login page that displays the message: “Temporary server error, please try again.”  When I attempt to log in using the same credentials I use for the HPC, it does not allow me to log back in. I have also tried Chrome and Safari, and seem to run into the same login page. My models still run, but I would like to be able to access the R page while they are running so I can see its progress. Thank you!

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RStudio / Interactive Session
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: Yes
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with connecting to RStudio Server while running an interactive session on the HPC [1]. The error message "Temporary server error, please try again" is displayed when attempting to connect to the RStudio Server [4]. Similar issues have been reported in the past, and resolving the issue often involves trying a new session or checking the settings used to start the interactive session [1][3][4].

ACTION
Try requesting a new session to see if the issue persists [4]. If the problem continues, please share the settings used to start the interactive session, including the partition, number of hours, number of nodes, and any additional modules loaded [1][3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you please share a screenshot of the error message you are seeing when attempting to connect to the RStudio Server?
- Have you tried using the default modules (R and rstudio-server) when starting the interactive session [3]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your assistance. I have tried requesting a new session, and the issue still persists. I am using the default partition "desktop" and the default modules (R and rstudio-server). I have attached a screenshot of the error message I am seeing.
Best regards,
[User]

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When experiencing issues with RStudio Server, try requesting a new session before seeking further assistance [4].
- Always use the default modules (R and rstudio-server) when starting an interactive session unless otherwise specified [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-54003
[3] : HPCSSUP-74123
[4] : HPCSSUP-88569

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RFS Access / Credential Confusion
Technical Action Required: No (workflow clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to use a generated token/device password for RFS access on Windows. The correct fix is to ensure they are on the Cambridge VPN or network, follow the Windows RFS instructions, and use their normal UIS credentials for Access Blue rather than the VPN/device token.

ACTION
1) Confirm the user is on the Cambridge VPN or directly on the network.
2) Direct them to the Windows RFS guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html
3) Clarify that the username/password prompt is for normal UIS credentials, not the VPN/device token.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patricia,

Please make sure you are connected to the Cambridge VPN or the Cambridge network directly, and follow the Windows guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html

For this login prompt, you should use your normal UIS credentials rather than the generated VPN/device token.

Best regards,
HPC Support

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / Remote Desktop Launch Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The missing “Launch Remote Desktop” button can be caused by launch-form settings such as project or account selection, but the screenshots are needed to confirm the exact cause. The safest next step is to review the full launch settings and confirm which project or account is selected.

ACTION
1) Ask the user for a full screenshot of the launch settings if not already available.
2) Confirm that the correct project or account is selected in the Remote Desktop form.
3) If project selection is wrong, explain that the default project may not be appropriate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Pete,

Please send us the full launch settings for the Remote Desktop session, including the selected project/account, so that we can confirm what is preventing the expected button from appearing.

If the current screenshot does not show the full launch form, please send one that includes the relevant project/account fields.

Best regards,
HPC Support

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Access / Invitation Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user accidentally rejected the storage access invitation by clicking “Do not agree”. Helpdesk cannot reverse that state directly; the storage/data manager who originally added the user must re-add or re-invite them.

ACTION
1) Explain that the original invitation cannot simply be toggled back by helpdesk.
2) Direct the user to the Data Manager or project owner who granted access.
3) Ask that person to re-add/reinvite the user to the storage.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Maria,

In this case the Data Manager who added you to the storage will need to re-add or re-invite you. We cannot reverse that response directly from the helpdesk side.

Best regards,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Reinstatement
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has accidentally clicked "Do not agree" instead of "Agree" to the terms and conditions for accessing the Research Data Store, resulting in loss of access [1][2][3][4]. The resolution involves the data manager or owner re-adding the user to the project to reinstate access [1][2][3][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Inform the user that they need to be re-added by the data manager to regain access [1][2][3][4].
2. Provide the user with instructions on how to contact their data manager to request re-addition.
3. If necessary, offer to facilitate communication between the user and the data manager.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm the name and contact information of the data manager who initially added you to the project?
- Have you already contacted the data manager to request re-addition?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Maria,
You will need to be re-added by the data manager to regain access to the Research Data Store. Please contact your data manager and request that they re-add you to the project. If you need assistance with this process, please don't hesitate to ask.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The data manager or owner must re-add the user to reinstate access, as this is a self-service action that requires their approval [1][2][3][4].
- Do not attempt to manually override or bypass the terms and conditions acceptance process.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-29506
[2] : HPCSSUP-27304
[3] : HPCSSUP-23926
[4] : HPCSSUP-95178

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN Onboarding / SSH Access / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has been added to a DAWN project but is unsure how to access the service and what credentials to use. The correct onboarding path is to confirm whether the submitted public key has been linked to the DAWN account, complete the separate SSH MFA setup, then connect using the corresponding private key and the DAWN login host.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the public key submitted via the dashboard/email has been linked to the DAWN account.
2) Direct the user to the SSH MFA guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk
3) Provide the DAWN login command:
   `ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
4) Clarify that the SSH client must point to the private key paired with the public key they supplied.
5) Share the DAWN/PVC documentation:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Matteo,

If your public key has already been linked to the DAWN account, the next step is to complete the separate SSH MFA setup and then connect using:
`ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

If your SSH client asks for authentication, make sure it is using the private key that matches the public key you sent us.

Best regards,
HPC Support
